raw_response:
SOC 2 – CC6.1 Readiness Assessment  
Observation period: Q4 2025  

----------------------------------------------------------------------
CONTROL GAPS & FINDINGS
----------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Human IAM User in AWS
• Policy Section: 3.3 Privileged Access ( “Direct IAM user accounts in AWS are prohibited for human users.” )  
• Gap: IAM CSV lists “david.kim” as an active IAM user with a console password and no access keys; this contradicts the policy requirement that all human access be federated through AWS SSO.  
• Severity: HIGH  

2. Root Account Interactive Use
• Policy Section: 3.3 Privileged Access (root credentials kept in safe; use requires two-person approval)  
• Gap: Root account password is enabled and was last used 2025-11-02. No evidence of the required two-person authorization or CloudTrail extract was provided. Interactive use of the root account without documented approval violates the policy and AWS best practice.  
• Severity: HIGH  

3. MFA Not Universally Enforced
• Policy Section: 4.2 Multi-Factor Authentication  
• Gap: Okta “Default Policy” (priority 3) explicitly sets requireFactor = false for any app not matched by higher policies. This leaves a technical path to access corporate or new production apps without MFA, conflicting with the policy statement “MFA… is required for any system accessing production data.”  
• Severity: HIGH  

4. Untimely User Off-Boarding – Tom Bradley & Priya Sharma
• Policy Section: 6.1 Termination (Okta disable within 4 hours of notification; checklist within 48 hours)  
• Gap:  
  – Tom Bradley: HR notice 2025-12-23, Okta disabled 2025-12-27 (≈ 96 hrs late); checklist still open.  
  – Priya Sharma: HR notice 2025-11-24, Okta disabled 2025-11-25 (≈ 24 hrs late).  
Both exceed the 4-hour revocation window and show incomplete documentation, creating lingering access risk.  
• Severity: HIGH  

5. Quarterly Access Review for Okta Exceeded 15-Day Window
• Policy Section: 5.1 Quarterly Access Reviews (15 business-day completion)  
• Gap: Review AR-2025-Q4-008 (Okta) initiated 2025-12-01, completed 2026-01-08 (≈ 28 business days). The review missed the mandated completion window.  
• Severity: MEDIUM  

6. Access Review Remediation Not Fully Executed
• Policy Section: 5.2 Review Process ( “Flagged access is revoked within 5 business days.” )  
• Gap: In AR-2025-Q4-008, 5 users were flagged but only 3 were revoked. Evidence of disposition for the remaining 2 users is missing, indicating incomplete remediation.  
• Severity: MEDIUM  

7. Aged & Unrotated Access Keys (> 90 Days)
• Policy Section: 4.3 Password and Credential Standards (automatic rotation every 90 days)  
• Gap:  
  – backup-s3-sync: Key 1 last rotated 2025-08-20 (≈ 130 days old).  
  – legacy-monitoring: Key 1 last rotated 2024-03-10 (≈ 659 days old).  
  – jenkins-legacy: Key 1 last rotated 2023-07-20 (≈ 526 days old).  
Keys exceed the 90-day rotation requirement.  
• Severity: MEDIUM  

8. Legacy Service Accounts Missing Owners & Quarterly Review
• Policy Section: 8 Service Accounts ( “must be registered in the CMDB with a designated owner” and “included in quarterly access reviews” )  
• Gap: CMDB shows “legacy-monitoring” and “jenkins-legacy” with Owner = Unassigned and last review dates 2024-09-15 and 2023-12-10, respectively—outside the observation quarter and lacking accountable ownership.  
• Severity: MEDIUM  

9. Service Accounts No Longer in Use but Still Active
• Policy Section: 8 Service Accounts / 5.2 Review Process (least privilege; revoke unused accounts)  
• Gap: “jenkins-legacy” last used 2024-08-15 but remains active with an aged key; “legacy-monitoring” is slated for migration to Datadog yet is still active. This increases attack surface and violates least-privilege expectations.  
• Severity: MEDIUM  

10. Access-Key Rotation Occurring on Only One Key
• Policy Section: 4.3 Password and Credential Standards (automatic rotation)  
• Gap: “backup-s3-sync” shows Key 2 active and rotated 2024-01-15, but Key 1 remains active and older than 90 days. Rotation should cover all active keys or old keys should be deactivated.  
• Severity: LOW  

11. MFA Prompt Lifetime for Corporate Apps (12 hrs) vs. “Always” Requirement
• Policy Section: 4.2 Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA required for any system accessing production data; factorPromptMode = ALWAYS for production)  
• Gap: “Corporate Apps – Standard MFA” policy sets factorPromptMode = SESSION with a 720-minute (12-hour) lifetime. If these apps (e.g., Jira) host tickets with production credentials or diagrams, the policy may not meet the “always prompt” expectation for sensitive data.  
• Severity: LOW  

12. Missing Two-Person Authorization Evidence for Root Credential Storage
• Policy Section: 3.3 Privileged Access (root use requires two-person authorization)  
• Gap: No artifact (safe log, approval record, or CloudTrail evidence) showing the two-person process for the 2025-11-02 root login.  
• Severity: LOW (separate from Finding 2; this is an evidence gap that may escalate severity if unresolved).  

----------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY

Meridian Cloud Systems has a comprehensive logical-access policy, but actual practice shows notable deviations—particularly around AWS human IAM usage, timely off-boarding, access-key hygiene, and universal MFA enforcement. High-severity items should be remediated before the formal SOC 2 examination period to reduce audit exceptions under CC6.1.
